observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management instruct the:
a) Municipal Accountant (MA) to (i) constantly monitor the book
reconciling items identified in the BRS and record immediately the items
requiring adjustments in the books; and (ii) restore to the CIB account
the total amount of the stale checks and unreleased/unclaimed checks as
of December 31, 2024 by debiting the Cash in Bank – LCCA account and
credit the appropriate Payables or Liabilities account to reflect the
correct balances of the affected accounts at year-end; and
b) AMT to ensure that the Inventory of Unclaimed Checks submitted to the
MAO includes all unreleased/unclaimed checks as of December 31 of each
year as basis in the preparation of JEV to record the restoration of its
total amount to the cash in bank and the accounts payable balances at
year-end and reversal of said entry in the ensuing year.

We recommended the Management to require the:
a) GSO to i) prepare and submit the RPCI using the prescribed template; ii)
maintain stock cards per for each commodity/stock; and iii) submit
regularly the SSMI to the MAO and reconcile accordingly; and
b) MA to i) require the submission of the SSMI and reconcile regularly with
the GSO; ii) maintain supplies ledger cards per for each
commodity/stock; iii) coordinate in writing with the concerned Offices to
enable the submission of the SSMI for the issued inventories totaling
₱3,806,744.51, otherwise; iv) record the issued inventories lacking SSMI
to the accountability of the concerned accountable officer and/or end-
user; and (v) ensure that JEVs for the recording of issued/expended
inventories are only based or supported by a SSMI.

We recommended the Management to instruct the:
a) MA to i) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify the
PPE and CIP totaling ₱3,764,152.94 to its proper accounts; ii) comply
strictly with the proper classification of PPE prescribed under the
RCALGU and recognition criteria of IPSAS 17 in order to attain fair
presentation thereof in the financial statements; and iii) ensure that any
additions or improvements to single PPE items are consolidated and
reflected within the main PPE item, rather than listed as additional PPE
item;
b) MA to compute and take up the depreciation expenses of the current
year, update the related accumulated depreciation accounts as well as
record adjustments in the government equity account for unrecorded
prior years' depreciation;
c) MA to conduct a thorough review of all PPE items with negative carrying
values and correct the erroneous depreciation entries to reflect accurate
asset valuations;
d) Inventory Committee (IC) to reconcile completely their records with the
MAO to correct the reported balances of PPE at year-end. Any
discrepancies between the RPCPPE and the accounting records should be
investigated and adjusted accordingly;
e) Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the immediate assessment
and disposal of the identified unserviceable properties;
f) GSO to exert efforts in determining/identifying the officers/employees
accountable for the reported “not found” PPE items and require the
concerned Department Heads or the identified accountable persons to
present “not found” items or submit a satisfactory explanation,
otherwise, hold them liable for the money value of the lost items unless
they file a request for relief from property accountability within the
prescribed period; and
g) MA together with the IC to avail the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts
pursuant to COA Circular 2020-006 to establish PPE balances that are
verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We reiterated our recommendations to the Management to instruct the:
a) MAg to (i) furnish the MAO with complete documents relative to
additions, distribution, reproduction and deaths of the breeding stocks
and reconcile the records related thereto with the MAO for proper
valuation as well as to confirm the existence of such; (ii) prepare
regularly and submit to the Audit Team the annual inventory of the
Biological Assets; and (iii) secure from the former MAg the complete
documentary requirements relative to the request for relief from
accountability as enumerated under Sections 151 and 152 of COA
Circular 92-386; and
b) MA to (i) book-up the necessary adjustments for the births, distributions,
deaths and changes in fair market value of the Biological Assets account
and thereafter, prudently apply all of the accounting rules and
116
regulations prescribed under IPSAS 27 pertaining to these assets; (ii)
draw an adjusting entry to correct the unrelated charges recorded in the
Biological Assets account which do not conform with paragraph 9 of
IPSAS 27; (iii) draw a reversing entry recording back the reduction in the
Breeding Stocks account in the absence of an approved relief from
accountability; and (iv) ensure that the subsequent
dropping/derecognition of lost properties in the books are supported by a
duly approved request for relief from accountability, otherwise record
the same as receivable from the accountable officer.

We recommended that the Management instruct the MA to:
a) coordinate with the MT to require the concerned suppliers, officers and
employees to submit the necessary documents to support their claims;
and
b) revert the payables which have remained outstanding for at least two (2)
years and for which no actual claim has been filed, and accounts payable
which are undocumented or not covered by perfected contracts on record
to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund upon approval of the
Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to comply with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended the Management to:
a) require the former MPDC to file a request for relief from property
accountability and submit the required documents to support the
request, otherwise issue a demand letter for production of the PPEs
under his accountability and compel the immediate return of the items;
b) strengthen its property and supply records management to ensure that
all government-issued properties are properly documented and
supported with the required property records;
c) instruct the MA to revert the derecognition of the lost PPE of the former
MPDC; and
d) derecognize the subject properties only upon approval of the request for
relief from accountability.

We recommended that Management instruct the MT to:
a) intensify strategic measures of implementing current policies and
procedures in the collection such as, but not limited to, house to house
collections with the assistance of the concerned barangay officials,
posting of Notice of Delinquency to publicly accessible and newspaper of
general circulation and sending follow-up letters/notices of tax collection
to delinquent taxpayers at the same instilling their obligations to the
government;
b) consider formulating/devising new and stringent strategies and impose
civil remedies and other sanctions guided by the Local Government Code
in order to fully address the low collection rate; and
c) make representations with the Provincial Treasurer for other legal
actions to enforce collections of delinquent taxes as provided in the Local
Government Code.

We recommended that the Management:
a. instruct the MT to pursue continuously extensive collection of market
fees from delinquent stallholders to increase the income of the
Municipality by devising strategies to make these stallholders aware on
the regulations of the Municipality particularly in payment of their
obligations; and
b. impose properly the provisions on surcharges and penalties as
incorporated in the Municipal Ordinances of Market Operations.

We recommended that the Management:
a) prepare and submit a 5-year Business Plan which cover all relevant facets
of the LEE’s operations including management, operations, staffing, risk
analysis, marketing, and sales;
b) include in the ordinance, among others, the purpose and justification for
the LEE, capitalization and sources of financing, organization and
staffing, pricing policy, and sunset provision;
c) instruct the MA to (i) set up and maintain a special account in the GF for
each LEE; and (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting entries to recognize
126
the Due from LEE and Due to Other Funds in the GF and LEE books,
respectively;
d) ensure that that each LEE has its own separate AOB;
e) ensure the LEE AOB is included in the approved appropriation
ordinance of the GFAB.
f) comply with the implementing procedures for the continuation of existing
LEE as enumerated under Chapter 5 of the aforementioned manual;
g) conduct Semi-Annual Financial Self-Sufficiency Review (SAFSSR) of
each LEE and prepare the corresponding report; and
h) ensure that transfers to LEEs take the form of subsidies or advances from
the General Fund (GF) and are not used to finance the personal services
of LEE staff.

We recommended that the Management:
a) designate a duly bonded collecting officer for the KAPEC and GRC to
ensure compliance with COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2, s.2020 and PD
No. 1445;
b) discontinue the practice of assigning JO workers to receive collection for
KAPEC and HRC. Instead, JO workers should be limited to support
functions that do not involve cash handling;
c) stop the issuance of TRs and ensure that all collections are immediately
acknowledged by ORs (AF No. 51) at the time of payment;
d) direct the Tourism Office and MAO to reconcile their collection records
to identify and resolve discrepancies amounting ₱12,539.00 for GRC; and
e) direct the Tourism Office to retain copies of all issued TRs until such time
that a duly bonded collecting officer is designated in the GRC and
KAPEC.

We recommended the Management to: a) require the Municipal Accountant to (i) monitor constantly the book reconciling items identified in the BRS and make adjustments in the books; and (ii) restore to the CIB balance the total amount of the stale checks by debiting the Cash in Bank – LCCA account and crediting the Payables or Liabilities account to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts at year-end; and b) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) issue written notices to the payees at least one month before a check becomes stale; and (ii) submit the list of all unreleased and unclaimed checks as of year-end to the MAO as basis for the restoration of its total amount to the cash in bank and accounts payable balances and reversal of said entry in the ensuing year.

We reiterated our recommendations and the Management agreed to (a) exhaust continuously all possible means provided by law for the collection of loans receivable such as consider salary deduction of said receivables for grantees who are existing municipal employees; (b) consider to request for write-off of the uncollectible amounts with the COA for other loans receivable which have been dormant and no other means of possible collection; and (c) consider imposing disciplinary sanctions against the Municipal Treasurer for repeatedly disregarding the recommendation to collect the long overdue receivables from employees of the MGG.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to (i) comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 and COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012 on the required analysis of dormant accounts by performing the regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors, accountable officers (Collecting Officers, Cashiers) for strict monitoring and disposition of said accounts; (ii) determine the amount of the receivables impaired and provide allowance for impairment of dormant receivables thereof in order to have fair presentation of the financial statements as required under IPSAS 29. Compute/determine impairment in accordance with the IPSAS or Philippine Financial Reporting Standards (PFRS); and (iii) consider the filing of request for write-off of the dormant receivable accounts with the Commission on Audit following the conditions and requirements in COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
Municipal Treasurer to continue sending demand letters to the accountable officials, employees and taxpayers with outstanding receivables particularly those that are more than ten years, and exhaust all means to collect dormant receivable accounts.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the PAO to address the discrepancies in the reciprocal accounts, Due from LGUs and Due to LGUs, and record the shares of barangays directly in their respective books; (ii) effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct amounts of receivable, payable and income accounts in the books at year-end; (iii) make representation with the Provincial Government for the immediate release/remittance of the amount due to the Municipality; (iv) analyze all the outstanding balances that are dormant, long outstanding and unsubstantiated for proper disposition and de-recognition, if warranted; and (v) communicate with source LGUs and perform reconciliation to ascertain the nature of the transferred funds, whether the funds received require liquidation or are to be treated as subsidy to the LGU; if due for liquidation, prepare and submit liquidation reports for the expended portion and cause the return to the source agencies of all unutilized balances of the fund transfers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) require the Municipal Accountant to (i) adopt consistently the perpetual inventory method and recognize inventories as asset at the time of purchase, and expense upon consumption of the inventories; (ii) update religiously their Supplies Ledger Card and keep track the actual Supplies Inventory on hand; and (iii) reconcile the balances of the inventory records with the records of the GSO; and (b) require the GSO/Inventory Committee to prepare and submit judiciously the RPCI in accordance with the prescribed format and deadline provided under Section 124 and Annex 48 of the MNGAS, respectively.

We reiterated to the Management the following: (a) create a Committee responsible for the registration of all real estate owned by the Municipality under the Torrens Title System pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386; and (b) upon completion of the land titling, require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary entries and record the cost incurred in the purchase or fair value if acquired through donation or transfers without cost of the land.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct a) the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments of the affected PPE accounts as a result of misclassification of the on-going and completed projects; b) the Municipal Engineer to provide promptly the MAO with copies of the report of completed projects and other necessary documents; and c) the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records regularly to determine the status and correct balances of the affected CIP and PPE accounts.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to instruct (a) the Inventory Committee under the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (GSO/MTO) to (i) fully reconcile their records with the MAO to correct the reported balances of PPE at year-end. Any discrepancies between the RPCPPE and the accounting records should be investigated and adjusted accordingly; and (ii) ensure that the submitted RPCPPE contain the complete required data as provided in Annex 49 of the MNGAS; (b) the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the immediate assessment and disposal of the identified unserviceable properties; and (c) the GSO to exert serious efforts in determining/identifying the officers/employees accountable for the reported “not found” PPE items and require the concerned Department Heads or the identified accountable persons to present the “not found” items or submit a satisfactory explanation, otherwise, hold them liable for the money value of the lost items unless they file a request for relief from property accountability within the prescribed period.

We reiterated our recommendations to the Management to instruct the:
Municipal Agriculturist to (i) furnish the MAO with complete documents relative to additions, distribution, reproduction and deaths of the breeding stocks and reconcile the records related thereto with the MAO for proper valuation as well as to confirm the existence of such; (ii) prepare regularly and submit to the Audit Team the annual inventory of  the Biological Assets; and (iii) secure from the former Municipal Agriculturist the complete documentary requirements relative to the request for relief from accountability as enumerated under Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular 92-386; and
Municipal Accountant to (i) book-up the necessary adjustments for the births, distributions, deaths and changes in fair market value of the Biological Assets account and thereafter, prudently apply all of the accounting rules and regulations prescribed under IPSAS 27 pertaining to these assets; (ii) draw an adjusting entry to correct the unrelated charges recorded in the Biological Assets account which do not conform with paragraph 9 of IPSAS 27; (iii) draw an adjusting entry returning back the reduction in the Breeding Stocks account in the absence of an approved relief from accountability; and (iv) ensure that the subsequent dropping/derecognition of lost properties in the books be supported by a duly approved request for relief from accountability, otherwise record the same as receivable from the accountable officer.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to instruct the a) Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to require the concerned suppliers, officers and employees to submit the necessary documents to support their claims otherwise, revert the amount to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund upon approval of the Sangguniang Bayan; (ii) refrain from recognizing payables out of services not actually rendered or in the absence of valid progress billings; and (iii) prepare the necessary adjustments to correct the erroneous recording of PPE and the corresponding liability account; and b) Municipal Engineer to ensure that obligation requests for infrastructure projects are supported with complete documentation to determine the actual services rendered by the corresponding contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) identify and establish the actual amount of the unexpended current and previous years’ DRRMF Preparedness and Mitigation Fund – CO and prepare necessary entries to return the said amount in the General Fund books; (b) identify properly and retain the Capital Outlay component of the unexpended DRRMF balance in the General Fund books; and (c) reconcile and effect corrections to the Notes to Financial Statements by reflecting the proper balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (i) comply strictly with the RCALGU in recording transactions of the Municipality as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009; (ii) reclassify the remaining misclassified inventory, if any, to proper accounts; and (iii) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to implement and adapt the RCALGU in the preparation of the Annual Budget to avoid conflict with the Municipal Accounting Office in the classification of accounts.

We reiterated the following courses of action:
a) strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated
December 19, 2016 and COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28,
2012 on the required analysis of dormant accounts by performing the
regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence
of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors, accountable
officers (Collecting Officers, Cashiers) for strict monitoring and
disposition of said accounts;
b) continue sending demand letters to the accountable officials, employees
and taxpayers with outstanding receivables particularly those of more
than ten years, and exhaust all means to collect dormant receivable
accounts;
c) determine the amount of the receivables impaired and provide allowance
for impairment of dormant receivables thereof in order to have fair
presentation of the financial statements as required under IPSAS 29.
Compute/determine impairment in accordance with the Philippine Public
Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) or Philippine Financial Reporting
Standards (PFRS); and
d) comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No.
2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the possible request for the write
off of the specified dormant receivables accounts to the Commission on
Audit.

Premises presented, we recommended and the Management agreed to (a)
instruct the Inventory Committee to thoroughly conduct and submit the Report on
Physical Count of Inventory as required regularly; (b) require the Municipal
Accountant to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards and ensure proper practice of the
perpetual inventory method in recording of inventories; and (c) require the General
Services Officer to maintain the appropriate Stock Cards required by the Manual
for all the supplies and materials of the Municipality for proper and centralized
monitoring.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated the following recommendations:
a) MAO and Inventory Team under the GSO/MTO to continuously
reconcile their records to correct the reported balances of PPE at year-
end. Any discrepancies between the RPCPPE and the accounting records
should be investigated and adjusted accordingly;
b) Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the immediate assessment
and disposal of the identified unserviceable properties; and
c) GSO to exert serious efforts in determining/identifying the
officers/employees accountable for the reported “not found” PPE items
and require the concerned Department Heads or the identified
accountable persons to present the “not found” items or submit a
satisfactory explanation, otherwise, hold them liable for the money value
of the lost items unless they file a request for relief from property
accountability within the prescribed period.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the Management to (a) create a
Committee which will be responsible for the registration of all real estate owned by
the Municipality under the Torrens Title System pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-
386; (b) upon completion of the land titling, require the Municipal Accountant to
prepare the necessary entries and record the cost incurred in the purchase or fair
value if acquired through donation or transfers without cost of the land, and (c)
direct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that the
Municipality has absolute or legal ownership over the properties before
implementing the construction of buildings and other structures.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the former
Municipal Agriculturist to immediately submit the complete documentary
requirements relative to the request for relief of accountability as enumerated under
Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular 92-386.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the Management agreed to:
a. require the GSO to: (i) identify insurable properties of the Municipality
taking into consideration the current condition or serviceability of each
property, those which are not serviceable must not be included; (ii)
submit to GSIS all the identified insurable properties owned by the
Municipality by filling up the Property Inventory Form (PIF) following
the guidelines set on Commission on Audit Circular No. 2018-002 dated
May 31, 2018; and (iii) include in their Annual Investment Plan the cost
of premiums required for the insurance of these properties; and
b. ensure that properties to be acquired by the Municipality in the future
will be properly insured.

In view of the above observations, we recommended the Management to:
a) institute the necessary remedies provided under RIRR of RA No. 9184,
such as but not limited to, imposition of liquidated damages for the
failure of the contractor to satisfactory complete the work within the
time specified in the contract;
b) conduct assessment for the remaining unfinished work and initiate the
immediate take-over process for the terminated project in order to
facilitate the completion of the project; and
c) intensify monitoring and supervision of project implementation in order
to avoid such recurrence for other municipal projects.

In view of the above representations, we reiterated the following
recommendations to the Management:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to analyze all the Inter-Agency
Receivables and Inter-Agency Liabilities that are dormant, long outstanding and
unsubstantiated for proper disposition and de-recognition, if warranted;
b. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the pertinent documents in
order to facilitate the immediate return of the remaining balance of fund
transfers to the source agencies;
c. instruct the Accounting and Treasury departments to continue
undertaking measures for the obligatory remittance of the eventual
unexpended balances to the National Treasury as required under
Executive Order No. 431, Section 122 of GAAM, Volume I and Sections
4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013; and
d. communicate with source agencies and perform reconciliation to
ascertain the nature of the transferred funds, whether the funds received
require liquidation or are to be treated as subsidy to the LGU; if due for
liquidation, prepare and submit liquidation reports for the expended
portion and cause the return to the source agencies or BTr of all
unutilized balances of the fund transfers.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following courses of
action:
a) require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly intensify measures of the
current policies and procedures being implemented by the Management
like the house to house collection, posting of Notice of Delinquency to
publicly accessible and conspicuous place such as entrance of Municipal
Building, Markets and the likes, for a wider dissemination of the notice
and sending follow-up letters/notices of tax collection to delinquent
taxpayers to remind them of their tax obligations;
b) consider formulating/devising new and rigid strategies and impose civil
remedies and other sanctions guided by the Local Government Code in
order to fully address the low collection rate; and
c) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to make representations with the
Provincial Treasurer for other actions needed to enforce collections of
delinquent taxes as provided in the Local Government Code.

In view of the above, the Audit Team recommended and the Management
agreed to:
a. instruct the MTO to furnish the MAO, on periodic basis, the necessary
report/document pertinent to collectibles from market rentals and
operation of waterworks system for recording purposes;
b. require the MAO make the necessary adjustment in the books for the
unrecorded receivables from unpaid market rights and rentals together
with the water fees;
c. make representation with the interested vendor if he/she is still willing to
take the “reserved” stall otherwise, offer it to other interested applicants
in order to earn the supposed equivalent income of the subject stall; and
d. require the Market Inspector and the Municipal Treasurer to submit
satisfactory explanation for entertaining reservation of market stalls in
the absence of legal basis.

The Audit Team recommended that the Management:
a. identify seminars/trainings that are vital in the municipal government’s
operation and that only specific target participants are authorized to
attend such;
b. adopt policies and procedures to be observed by each participant while
attending seminars and other similar activity conducted within the
locality aimed to minimize absenteeism, tardiness and unnecessary
disruptions;
c. strictly observe the call for austerity as provided under Administrative
Order No. 6, s. 2017 and consider possibility that undertaking trainings,
seminar-workshops, conventions or similar activities where the required
training facilities are locally available shall be held in the province; and
d. ensure that the provisions of Section 2.2 of DILG Memorandum Circular
No. 2019 - 82 and Section 2.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-003 are adhered
to by the officials of the Municipal Government relative to
participation/attendance to various conventions, seminars and other
activities.

The Audit Team recommended that the Management:
a. require the issuance of government official receipt or Accountable Form No.
51 to acknowledge payment of registration/seminar fees to government
organizations to fully establish accountability;
b. ensure that the provisions of Section 505 of RA No. 7160 and Section 2.0 of
COA Circular No. 2012-003 are adhered to by the officials of the Municipal
Government relative to participation/attendance to various conventions,
seminars and other activities of VMLP, PCL and Liga ng mga Barangay sa
Pilipinas; and
c. strictly observe the call for austerity as provided under AO No. 6, s. 2017

We recommended the Management to a) require the immediate refund of the
total amount of ₱394,625.00 representing payment of salaries, allowances and other
benefits received by the said official as Municipal Civil Registrar; b) strictly adhere to
the CSC policies, guidelines and standards on hiring and appointing agency
personnel specifically the provisions stipulated under the Memorandum Circular
No. 40, s. 1998 also known as Revised Omnibus Rules on Appointments and other
Personnel Actions; and c) require the Human Resource Management Office to
firmly screen applicants to ensure that they meet the CSC requirements for the
position so that the appointing authority is properly guided in selecting the
applicants who can efficiently discharge the duties and responsibilities of the
position.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the LDRRMO to
prepare the LDRRM Investment Plan in standard format required in COA
Circular No. 2012-002 incorporate under a separate caption the PPAs chargeable to
the unexpended balances of prior years for approval of the local sanggunian and the
Local Chief Executive.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the Management agreed to:
a) require the HRMO to evaluate staffing pattern and consider the necessity
to hire the additional two mandatory staff requirement as provided in
Section 12(b) in RA 10121 and Item 4.2 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC No.
2014-1 who will carry out the specified functions stated therein and
publish the notice of vacancies; and
b) instruct the LDRRMO to maximize the utilization of LDRRM fund to
achieve the purpose for which the fund was created.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the Management agreed to:
a) ensure that charges to the LDRRM fund are supportive of the disaster
risk management activities as provided in Section 21 of Republic Act No.
10121 such as pre-disaster preparedness programs including trainings,
purchasing life-saving rescue equipment, supplies and medicines for post-
disaster activities and for the payment of premiums on calamity
insurance;
b) provide appropriation for LDRRM office from General Fund proper
necessary in the conduct of its day to day operations; and
c) make sure that revisions and any adjustments made are authorized by
the LDRRMC in accordance to Section 12(c) of Republic Act No. 10121

Premised presented, we recommended and the Management agreed to
instruct the LDRRMO to strictly adhere to the proper procurement and
distribution process of welfare goods, together with the MAO and GSO to maintain
Supplies Ledger Cards and Stock Cards for proper recording and distribution of
relief goods as required by COA Circular No. 2014-002.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the Management agreed to
instruct the LDRRMO and Municipal Accountant to prepare, certify and submit
monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF to the Audit Team as
required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management (a) instruct the
Municipal Engineer and the MPDC to fully utilize the allotted budget for the socio-
economic and environmental development and implement immediately all projects
as programmed to provide benefits to its constituents; and (b) strictly allocate the
20% DF on programs/projects which partake the nature of investment or capital
expenditures in accordance with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No.
2017-1.

In view of the above representations, we recommended the Management to:
a) direct the GAD focal person/official, with the aim of ensuring that the
genuine local gender issues will be addressed, to conduct study, data
gathering, and coordination with barangays, social welfare/development
official/s and the Philippine National Police to identify activities that
affect the residents, especially the welfare of women and/or violations
committed against them that are vital for the formulation of
programs/projects that have to be in accordance with the provisions of
the Republic Act;
b) update the local GAD code to ensure up-to-date gender-responsive local
ordinances and policies that pertains to the promotion, among others, of
the welfare, rights, economic status and physical health of girls, women
and elderly women; and
c) observe strict adherence to the procedures and guidelines set forth in the
IRR of RA 9710 in order to attain/achieve the objective of the “Magna
Carta of Women”.

We recommended that the Management direct the concerned municipal
officials and the District Supervisor to (a) require the Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Accountant to regularly submit to the MSBG reports on Quarterly SEF
(RPT) collection and the projected annual SEF income for the year to be used as
basis for the SEF budget of the ensuing year (b) prepare the MSB budget not later
than October 31 of the current year as required under Section 5.5 of the Revised
Guidelines on the Use of Special Education Fund; and (c) implement the programs
and projects allocated out of the approved Municipal School Board budget so that
the needed instructional materials, equipment, services and facilities will be made
available to pupils and students of the elementary and secondary public schools as
well as the vocational/technical and non-formal schools.

Based on the premises cited above, we reiterated our recommendations to the
Management to:
a) require the members of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to
exert effort in revising the ten-year SWM plan consistent with the NSWM
framework with complete supporting documents and submit the same to
the NSWMC for approval;
b) ensure that the MRF in every barangay are established in accordance
with the prescribed specifications and ensure proper utilization after
their completion; and
c) undertake necessary steps to ensure the Municipality’s compliance with
the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 for a more comprehensive solid
waste management programs necessary for the attainment of total
protection of the environment and general welfare of the
public/constituents.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to
submit the copies of contracts/purchase orders together with the supporting
documents, within five (5) working days from perfection thereof pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2009-001, for prompt evaluation by Auditor and to point out to
management the defects of the orders/contracts and the project itself, should there
be any, for them to finally effect timely corrective measures.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the Management agreed to
(a) take the necessary actions to expedite the collection of the unsettled
disallowances; (b) ensure that before allowing settlement of disallowances through

officers, who took part in the disbursement of disallowed expenditures, to assume
responsibility in the final settlement of the remaining balance of the disallowance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the (a) Municipal Accountant make the necessary
adjusting entries for the staled checks by  restoring  to  Cash-in-Bank  and
Accounts Payable the  amount  equivalent  to  the  staled  checks  and;  (b)
Municipal Treasurer issue written notice to the payees availability  of  the
checks at least one month before their checks become stale.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to:

     a) effect the immediate closure of the depository  accounts  with  PNB
        Boac Branch  and  transfer  its  balance  to  an  existing  account
        maintained with LBP Boac Branch; and


     b) observe compliance with the provisions of Local Government Code (RA
        7160) and Department of Finance  (DOF)  orders  on  maintenance  of
        depository accounts.

We recommended the following courses of action:


     a) require the Municipal Treasurer to devise  a  strategy  that  would
        result to enhanced collection system;


     b) intensify tax collection campaign through personal  follow-ups  and
        sending notices of tax collection to delinquent taxpayers to remind
        them of their tax obligations;

     c) coordinate/make representations  with  Punong  Barangays  and  seek
        their  assistance  for  them  (barangay  officials)  to  personally
        deliver the above notices/letters to concerned individuals  in  the
        locality and then personally advise/oblige  them  to  settle  their
        accounts; and

     d) strictly enforce the provisions of the Revenue Code.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated the following
recommendations:

     a) initiate the revision of the MOA by adding provisions pertinent  to
        penalties and sanctions;


     b) direct the Municipal  Treasurer  to  devise  remedial  measures  to
        facilitate higher collection efficiency;


     c) require the  Municipal  Agriculturist  to  strictly  supervise  and
        monitor the implementation  of  the  program  and  prepare  monthly
        reports to guarantee  the  regular  monitoring  and  determine  the
        success of the project; and

     d) seek advice from a  Legal  Officer  on  the  possible  recovery  of
        collection of past  due  accounts  including  penalties  and  other
        charges thereof.

We recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  instruct  the  (a)
Municipal  Accountant  to  stop  processing  transaction  without   complete
documentation and proper approval; (b) Municipal Treasurer not  to  pay  and
instead return the voucher to  the  accounting  office  for  completion  and
approval of documents; and (c) observe COA Circular 2012-001 dated June  14,
2012 on the prescribed guidelines and documentary  requirements  in  payment
of government transactions.

We recommended and Management  agreed  to  the  following  courses  of
      action:


     a) strictly comply with the provisions of COA  Circular  No.  2016-005
        dated December  19,  2016  and  COA  Circular  No.  2012-004  dated
        November 28, 2012 on the required analysis of dormant  accounts  by
        performing the regular  and  periodic  verification,  analysis  and
        validation of the existence of the receivables  and  determine  the
        concerned  debtors,  accountable  officers  (Collecting   Officers,
        Cashiers) for strict monitoring and disposition of said accounts;

     b) provide allowance for impairment of dormant receivables in order to
        have a fair presentation of the financial  statements  as  required
        under PPSAS 29. Compute/determine impairment in accordance with the
        Philippine  Public  Sector  Accounting  Standards   or   Philippine
        Financial Reporting Standards; and

     c) comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No.
        2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the possible request  for  the
        write off  of  the  specified  dormant  receivables  accounts  to  the
        Commission on Audit.

We recommended the following to the Management:

     a) require the MAO and General Services  Office  under  the  Municipal
        Treasurer’s Office (GSO/MTO) to reconcile their records to  correct
        the reported balances of PPE at year-end. Any  discrepancy  between
        the RPCPPE and the Accounting records should  be  investigated  and
        adjusted accordingly;


     b) require the GSO/MTO to strictly comply with the provisions  of  the
        Manual on the New Government Accounting System,  Volume  I  on  the
        submission  of  RPCPPE  specifically  our  reiteration  to  conduct
        complete physical count of PPEs; and

     c) create an  Appraisal  and  Disposal  Committee  to  facilitate  the
        assessment and disposal of the identified unserviceable properties.

In view of the foregoing observation,  we  recommended  the  following
courses of action:

     a) direct the BAC to  require  the  winning  supplier  to  submit  the
        dealer’s  permit  issued   by   the   National   Telecommunications
        Commission as provided by NTC  Memorandum  Circular  No.  09-08-91,
        otherwise said transaction be disallowed in audit;


     b) direct the MDRRMO to submit to the office the  required  Permit  to
        Purchase issued by the NTC-Region IV and written justification  for
        units not covered by an approved Permit to Purchase;

     c) require the BAC  and  BAC  Secretariat  to  submit  copies  of  the
        corresponding eligibility requirements as  enumerated  in  RA  9184
        necessary for the review and evaluation;

     d) require the BAC to submit written  justification  why  the  awarded
        contract for handheld radios which exceeded the  prevailing  market
        price should not be disallowed in audit; and

     e) adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October
        29, 2012, NTC Memorandum Circulars and provisions of  RA  9184  and
        its  Implementing  Rules  and   Regulation   in   procuring   radio
        communication equipment for the Municipality.

We recommended the following courses of action:


     a) require the submission of  the  complete  documentary  requirements
        relative to request for  relief  of  accountability  as  enumerated
        under Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular 92-386; and


     b) instruct  the  Municipal  Accounting  Office  to  effect  necessary
        adjusting entries only after the issuance of Credit Notice  arising
        from  the  result  of   the   evaluation   of   the   relief   from
        accountability.

We recommended the Management to:

     a) instruct the implementing offices to fast track  implementation  of
        projects funded by National Government Agencies   and  monitor  the
        implementation   of   the   projects   since    delayed     project
        implementation  results in delayed access of  the  constituents  to
        the intended purpose of the fund transfers; and


     b) require immediate remittance of the unexpended balances of projects
        that are already completed to the  National  Treasury  as  required
        under Executive Order No. 431, Section 122 of GAAM,  Volume  I  and
        Sections 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013.

The following are the Audit Team’s recommended courses of action:

     a) for contract to be valid, the Local Chief Executive is required  to
        secure authorization from the  Sangguniang  Bayan  before  entering
        into any contracts for the Municipality;


     b) in case there is a need for consultants,  engage  only  experts  in
        their  respective  fields   and   ensure   proper   assessment   of
        qualifications or specialization  vis-à-vis  functions,  tasks  and
        services to satisfy the actual necessity of the Municipality;

     c)  ensure  that  payments  were  properly  supported  with   adequate
        documentation, otherwise Notice of Suspension or Disallowance  will
        be served; and

     d) strictly adhere with the various rules and regulations, to wit: (i)
        RA 7160 particularly Sections 22 and 444, (ii) RA 9184 and its IRRs
        on  the  hiring  of  consulting  services,  (iii)  COA  rules   and
        regulations on Job Orders and Contract of Services and  (iv)  other
        pertinent rules and regulation related to Job Orders  and  Contract
        of Services.

We recommended the management to:

     a) stop  the  practice  of  splitting  single  project  into  multiple
        contracts; and


     b) require the BAC to  submit  (i)  a  written  explanation  why  they
        resorted to multiple award of contracts for  each  single  project;
        and  (ii)  a  report  on  the  pass/fail  criterion  made  on   the
        determination of the Lowest Calculated Bid in all  projects  listed
        above.

We recommended that management (a) enforce  strictly  the  regulations
pertaining to the grant of work suspension and extension  of  contract  time
by  closely  monitoring  the  project  and   meticulously   evaluating   the
reasons/purpose of their requests pursuant to the  provisions  of  RA  9184;
(b) initiate appropriate legal actions against  the  erring  contractor  for
incurring unwarranted slippages and not complying with terms and  conditions
of the contract specifically the attainment of a speedy  completion  of  the
project that will ensure its early utilization to  enable  the  municipality
as well as the  stallholders  to  derive  or  generate  income  out  of  its
operation; and (c) deduct from the succeeding claims of the  contractor  the
amount of liquidated damages corresponding to 289 days delay  in  completion
of the project.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated the following recommendations:

     a) adhere strictly to the guidelines  provided  under  DILG-DBM  Joint
        Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February  22,  2017  to  ensure
        efficient output, come up with projects with  developmental  impact
        that would improve more the socio-economic condition of the greater
        number of its constituents;


     b) identify the programs and projects that  must  be  prioritized  for
        inclusion and consideration in the  Annual  Investment  Plan.   For
        those  which  are  inconsistent  with  the  governing   rules   and
        regulations, initiate amendments or realignment of  the  latter  to
        those that address the needs of the constituents as far as tangible
        projects and programs are concerned; and

     c) source and appropriate in the General Fund Proper expenses that are
        administrative in nature which meet  the  validity  and  regularity
        requirements under existing accounting and auditing rules.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:

     a) instruct the Municipal Budget  Officer  to  allocate  the  required
        amount equivalent to one  percent  (1%)  of  the  Internal  Revenue
        Allotment (IRA) for the strengthening  and  implementation  of  the
        programs of the LCPC; and


     b) observe provisions of Republic Act (RA)  9344  also  known  as  the
        Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act of 2006.

Based on the premises cited above, we recommended the management to:

     a) direct the LDRRMO and the Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the
        rest of barangays that still have  to  establish  their  respective
        material  recovery  facilities  and  provide  them  with  technical
        assistance to facilitate the  establishment/construction  of  their
        own  for  them  to  participate  in  the  proper  disposal  of  the
        municipality’s wastes/garbage;

     b) ensure that the facilities in every  barangay  are  established  in
        accordance  with  the  prescribed  specifications  and   they   are
        guaranteed to be operational after their completion; and

     c) undertake steps in the end that  the  municipality  will  be  fully
        compliant with the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 for  a  more
        comprehensive  solid  waste  management  program  to   maintain   a
        pollution-free municipality.

observations.managementAction: 14 out of the 25 barangays are still not compliant with the provisions of RA 9003.
observations.reason: For CY 2016, there are still 11 barangays with MRFs Reiterated under Finding No. 12 in CY 2016 AAR (pages 59 to 60)

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to the Management:

     a) The MAO and General Services Office under the Municipal Treasurer’s
        Office (GSO/MTO) should  immediately  reconcile  their  records  to
        correct the reported balances of PPE at year-end. Any discrepancies
        between  the  RPCPPE  and  the   accounting   records   should   be
        investigated and adjusted accordingly;


     b) Require the GSO/MTO to strictly comply with the provisions  of  the
        Manual on the New Government Accounting System,  Volume  I  on  the
        submission  of  RPCPPE  specifically  our  reiteration  to  conduct
        complete physical count of PPEs; and


     c) Create an  Appraisal  and  Disposal  Committee  to  facilitate  the
        assessment and disposal of the identified unserviceable properties.

We recommended the Management to:

     a) Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Disbursing Officer  not  to
        draw cash advances in excess of the allowed maximum  accountability
        unless they had  already  a  duly  approved  higher  fidelity  bond
        appropriate for their required cash advances;


     b) Ensure that no cash advances will  be  granted  in  excess  of  the
        maximum accountability allowed in the bond.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following:

     a) Initiate the revision of the MOA by adding provisions pertinent  to
        penalties and sanctions;


     b) Direct the Municipal  Treasurer  to  devise  remedial  measures  to
        facilitate higher collection efficiency;

     c) Require the  Municipal  Agriculturist  to  strictly  supervise  and
        monitor the implementation  of  the  program  and  prepare  monthly
        reports to guarantee  the  regular  monitoring  and  determine  the
        success of the project; and

     d) Seek advice from a  Legal  Officer  on  the  possible  recovery  of
        collection of past  due  accounts  including  penalties  and  other
        charges thereof.

In view of the above, we recommended the management to:

     a) Exert utmost effort for the proper representation or dialogue  with
        the source agencies in view of  the  unimplemented  projects  whose
        funds are not viable to succeed due to high cost of  materials  and
        request approval from  them  to  use  the  fund  to  augment  other
        priority programs and projects of the municipality;

     b) Instruct the  Accounting  and  Treasury  Departments  to  undertake
        measures for the obligatory remittance of the  eventual  unexpended
        balances to the National Treasury as required under Executive Order
        No. 431, Section 122 of GAAM, Volume I and Sections 4.9 and 6.7  of
        COA Circular No. 94-013.

Management Comment:

      During the exit conference,  the  Municipal  Accountant  informed  the
Audit Team  that  she  has  instructed  all  the  end-users  to  inform  the
Accounting Department of the submission of the liquidation reports for  them
to take-up proper adjustments in the books.  She signified  her  willingness
to comply  with  the  recommendations  in  cooperation  with  the  Municipal
Treasurer.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

      The Audit Team stands firm on  its  recommendations.   The  Management
was advised that upon remittance of  the  unexpended  balance  of  the  fund
releases/financial assistance to the National Treasury,  the  source  agency
should be furnished with the Official  Receipt  for  their  information  and
proper adjustment in their books.

We recommended the following courses of action:


     a) Require the Municipal Treasurer to devise  a  strategy  that  would
        result to enhanced collection system;


     b)  Intensify  tax  collection  campaign  through  sending   follow-up
        letters/notices of tax collection to delinquent taxpayers to remind
        them of their tax obligations;

     c) Coordinate/make representations  with  Punong  Barangays  and  seek
        their  assistance  for  them  (barangay  officials)  to  personally
        deliver the above notices/letters to concerned individuals  in  the
        locality and then personally advise/oblige  them  to  settle  their
        accounts, and

     d) Strictly enforce the provisions of the Revenue Code.

Management Comment:

      According to the Municipal Treasurer, they  continuously  send  demand
letters to delinquent tax payers to remind them of their obligation  but  to
no avail, the collection has not improved since then.  He  added  that  they
had submitted the List of Delinquent Tax Payers together with  the  copy  of
the Demand Letters to  the  Office  of  the  Provincial  Treasurer  for  the
imposition of appropriate remedies in accordance with  provisions  mentioned
above.

In view of the above, we recommended to seek the refund of the  amount
paid from the former Municipal Elective Officials that  represent  the  cash
equivalent of the five-day mandatory  leave  that  were  not  deducted  from
their respective vacation leave credits.

We  recommended  that  management  enforce   strictly   the   regulations
pertaining to the grant of work suspension and extension of contract time
by  closely  monitoring  the  project  and  meticulously  evaluating  the
reasons/purpose of their requests pursuant to the provisions of RA 9184.

      In addition, appropriate legal actions  should  be  initiated  by  the
management  against  the  erring  contractor   for   incurring   unwarranted
slippages and not complying  with  terms  and  conditions  of  the  contract
specifically the attainment of a speedy completion of  the  project  that
will ensure its early utilization to enable  the municipality  as  well  as
the stallholders to derive or generate income out of its operation.

In   view   of   the   foregoing,   we   reiterated   the   following
recommendations:

     a) Adhere strictly to the guidelines  provided  under  DILG-DBM  Joint
        Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1  dated  April  13,  2011  to  ensure
        efficient output, come up with projects with  developmental  impact
        that would improve more the socio-economic condition of the greater
        number of its constituents; and


     b) Identify, first and foremost, the programs and projects  that  must
        be prioritized  for  inclusion  and  consideration  in  the  Annual
        Investment  Plan.   For  those  which  are  inconsistent  with  the
        governing rules and regulations, initiate amendments or realignment
        of the latter to those that address the needs of  the  constituents
        as far as tangible projects and programs are concerned.

     c) Source and appropriate in the General Fund Proper expenses that are
        administrative in nature which meet  the  validity  and  regularity
        requirements under existing accounting and auditing rules.

In view of the above, we recommended the submission of copies of
contracts/purchase orders together with the supporting documents, within
five days from perfection thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001,
for prompt evaluation by Auditor and to point out to management the defects
of the orders/contracts and the project itself, should there be any, and to
finally effect timely corrective measures.

The management should require the  Municipal  Accountant  to  strictly
enforce  the  periodic  submission  of  these  disbursement   vouchers   and
supporting documents within the prescribed period.  She must look  into  the
root cause of delayed submission and immediately take the  necessary  action
to solve the problem.

In view of the above representations, we  recommended  and  Management
agreed to:

     a) Direct the GAD focal person/official, with the aim of ensuring that
        the genuine local gender  issues  will  be  addressed,  to  conduct
        study, data gathering,  and  coordination  with  barangays,  social
        welfare/development official/s and the Philippine  National  Police
        to identify activities that affect the  residents,  especially  the
        welfare of women and/or violations committed against them that  are
        vital for the formulation of programs/projects that have to  be  in
        accordance with the provisions of the republic act;


     b) Ensure that the Annual GAD Plan and Budget was approved by the DILG
        before incorporating to the Annual Budget so that the programs that
        will be  implemented  under  GAD  are  directed  to  improving  the
        municipal government’s capacity for  gender  mainstreaming,  gender
        issues and/or promoting gender equality; and

     c) Observe strict  adherence  to  the  guidelines  set  forth  in  the
        preparation of Annual GAD Plan and Budget as provided for under PCW-
        DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

This is a reiteration of prior year’s audit findings and recommendations.


Management Comment:

      During the exit conference, the GAD Focal Person admitted that the  CY
2016 GAD Plan was not issued a  Certificate  of  Approval  from  DILG.   She
justified that the preparation of said GAD Plan was delayed  due  to  change
in GAD Focal Person.  She assured the Audit Team of their compliance to  the
above recommendations.

Based on the premises cited above, we recommend the management to:

     a) Direct the LDRRMO and the Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the
        rest of barangays that still have  to  establish  their  respective
        material  recovery  facilities  and  provide  them  with  technical
        assistance to facilitate the  establishment/construction  of  their
        own  for  them  to  participate  in  the  proper  disposal  of  the
        municipality’s wastes/garbage;

     b) Ensure that the facilities in every  barangay  are  established  in
        accordance  with  the  prescribed  specifications  and   they   are
        guaranteed to be operational after their completion; and

     c) Undertake steps in the end that  the  municipality  will  be  fully
        compliant with the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 for  a  more
        comprehensive  solid  waste  management  program  to   maintain   a
        pollution-free municipality.
